ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a Japanese nabe sea bass set meal. Mapped to closest parent 'fish dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the nearest defensible ancestor. The composite set meal nature (hot pot fish, rice, sushi, tempura) has no direct FoodOn equivalent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish and fish products) used as the closest defensible parent category. No FoodEx2 code exists for a Japanese nabe set meal composite dish. Sea bass (suzuki) is the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Japanese nabe sea bass set meal composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 700 g, comprising nabe sea bass hot pot, steamed white rice, assorted sushi pieces, and tempura with dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimate derived from: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159); White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Fish, mixed species, cooked (FDC ID 175168); Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, fried (FDC ID 171477) as tempura analogue
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB entry exists for a composite Japanese nabe sea bass set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components: sea bass cooked dry heat (FDC ID 175159) ~35% by weight, steamed white rice cooked (FDC ID 169756) ~30%, raw fish sushi component (FDC ID 175168) ~20%, and tempura battered fried component ~15%. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were used as a cross-reference and are broadly consistent with the USDA composite estimate. Values have been retained from the input with minor rounding adjustments for internal consistency. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and detailed micronutrient data are set to -1 as a full composite breakdown is not available from a single approved source entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite Japanese nabe sea bass set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate sources: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) and sushi rice (GI ~55–68, Brand-Miller et al. 2008). The protein from sea bass and fat from tempura moderate the overall glycaemic response, yielding a blended estimate of GI 65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 79.8) / 100 ≈ 52.0 g, where carbohydrate per 700 g serving = 11.4 × 7 = 79.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 314 mg per 100 g; a 700 g serving delivers approximately 2,200 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and dashi broth, avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table, and limit the tempura component, as a full set meal serving can approach or exceed the recommended daily sodium ceiling of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (11.4 g per 100 g; approximately 80 g per full serving) from refined sushi rice and steamed white rice with a blended GI of approximately 65.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size of the rice and sushi components, request minimal tempura batter to reduce refined carbohydrate load, and consider substituting brown rice where available to lower the glycaemic impact of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.4 g per 100 g and sea bass provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, but sodium at 314 mg per 100 g and deep-fried tempura components elevate cardiovascular risk at full serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit the tempura component to reduce saturated and trans fat intake, request reduced soy sauce to manage sodium, and favour the nabe sea bass and vegetable components which provide omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiac health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sea bass and sushi fish components provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; however, tempura generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and soy sauce sodium may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit the deep-fried tempura component to reduce AGE exposure, request reduced soy sauce, and monitor response to eggplant as a nightshade vegetable that may trigger sensitivity in some RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Nabe Sea Bass Set Meal (鍋スズキ定食, Nabe Suzuki Teishoku) is a comprehensive Japanese set meal served in Japanese restaurants in Singapore, featuring sea bass (suzuki) prepared as a nabe (Japanese hot pot or stew) as the centrepiece, accompanied by steamed white rice, assorted sushi pieces, and tempura with dipping sauce. The nabe component involves simmering sea bass fillets in a dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, together with tofu, mushrooms, napa cabbage, and other vegetables, producing a savoury, umami-rich hot pot with tender, moist fish. The sushi component typically includes nigiri or maki with raw fish such as salmon or tuna, while the tempura component features lightly battered and deep-fried seafood or vegetables. The overall flavour profile is delicate and savoury with contrasting textures: silky hot pot broth, firm sea bass flesh, vinegared sushi rice, and crisp tempura batter. Nutritionally, the set meal provides approximately 135.7 kcal per 100 g, with 9.3 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 11.4 g carbohydrate. Sea bass is a lean white fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids, supporting cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. Sodium is elevated at 314 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and dashi broth, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The refined carbohydrate content from sushi rice and steamed white rice gives the meal a medium glycaemic index of approximately 65, relevant for type 2 diabetes management. The set meal is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, soy, or gluten allergies. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Japanese casual dining restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for a composite Japanese nabe sea bass set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite of the primary components (sea bass cooked dry heat FDC ID 175159, steamed white rice FDC ID 169756, raw fish component FDC ID 175168, tempura analogue). The input GrabFood ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with the USDA composite estimate and have been retained with minor rounding adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate sources from the University of Sydney GI Database and Brand-Miller et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this composite Japanese set meal is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026; confirmed by review of USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) accessed 2026-04-17. 2. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central component entries; the input GrabFood ai_generated values were used as a cross-reference only and are not cited as a primary source. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice and sushi rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Brand-Miller et al. (2008 Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists. 4. The set meal is served in Singapore Japanese restaurants; region_of_origin is listed as Japan with Singapore as the serving context. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed as dashi may contain non-halal bonito; is_halal is set to false pending restaurant-level certification. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.07 per 100 g is a trace amount from the tempura deep-frying component, consistent with USDA data for fried foods. 7. This is a Japanese cuisine dish; mandatory multilingual requirement is Japanese native-script name only (G6). chinese_simplified, chinese_traditional, malay, tamil, and hindi local_names are null as this dish is Japanese, not Singaporean, and those language fields are not mandated for Japanese cuisine records.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Nabe Sea Bass Set Meal', a precise canonical English name. Japanese local name 鍋スズキ定食 is present satisfying G6 for Japanese cuisine. Romanized form Nabe Suzuki Teishoku is also provided. This is a Japanese cuisine dish; G6 requires only Japanese native-script name.

Analogous estimate basis fully documented. glycemic_index is not 0 or null.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium-focused guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate, carbohydrate/GI-focused guidance with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, omega-3 and sodium guidance). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
